title: Applications of random matrix theory on PCA
speaker: Jun Yin

proof_stages.label: Full-rank deterministic deformations of Wigner matrices
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the problem of understanding H+A when H is a Wigner matrix and A is a full-rank deterministic symmetric matrix, emphasizing limiting density, local rigidity, eigenvector delocalization, outliers, and edge/bulk statistics.

proof_stages.label: Loss of Wigner isotropy and tractable special cases
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that Wigner matrices rely on entry independence and isotropic rows/columns, and that a general non-diagonal A destroys the usual row-column growth arguments except in diagonal-A or GOE-rotatable cases.

proof_stages.label: Sample-covariance and PCA deformation model
proof_stages.summary: The analogous covariance problem is set up for matrices of the form T X(I-ee^*)X^*T^*, derived from centered covariance estimation under a linear-mixing model y=Tx, with the statistical inverse problem being to infer information about TT^* from the sample matrix.

proof_stages.label: Spiked signal assumptions and outlier eigenvalue recovery
proof_stages.summary: Under assumptions of finitely many signal eigenvalues, comparable noise eigenvalues, and weak size comparability of m and n, the talk states the outlier threshold and location formula that allow the signal eigenvalue to be recovered from an observed outlier.

proof_stages.label: Outlier eigenvector alignment and delocalization
proof_stages.summary: For an outlier eigenvector u, the projection against any fixed vector w is shown to be a deterministic multiple of the signal projection plus a small error, yielding fixed signal angle, isotropy in orthogonal directions, mixing rules for close outliers, and sparse-support recovery.

proof_stages.label: Scope of the theorem package versus previous work
proof_stages.summary: The speaker compares the results with earlier work requiring diagonal, Gaussian, square, or fixed-spike assumptions, and lists the main outputs: optimal-scale rigidity, delocalization for non-outliers and outliers, some near-threshold information, Tracy-Widom edge laws, and four-moment universality.

proof_stages.label: Isotropic local laws and no-signal resolvent comparison
proof_stages.summary: The proof mechanism is to establish isotropic resolvent laws for H+A and the covariance analog, then compare the signal model to a no-signal matrix S by representing its resolvent in terms of no-signal resolvents controlled from arbitrary deterministic directions.

examples.name: Equal nonzero mean as a rank-one perturbation
examples.note: The speaker contrasts the hard full-rank deterministic deformation with the easy case where all means are equal, giving only a rank-one perturbation, and with prior finite-rank perturbation work.
examples.engagement_reason: He only notes that equal nonzero means give H plus a rank-one matrix and calls it easy, without doing the perturbation analysis.

passes.reason: The equal-mean matrix is a scalar all-ones rank-one perturbation, hence a collapsed low-rank and permutation-symmetric special case.
Equal means give an all-ones rank-one perturbation, a minimal nonzero low-rank and highly regular special case.
The equal-mean perturbation is the all-ones rank-one matrix, hence highly regular and low-rank, used only as an easy case.
the equal-mean perturbation is the all-ones rank-one matrix, a maximally permutation-regular minimal-rank nonzero case used only as an easy special case
The equal-mean perturbation is the all-ones rank-one matrix, hence permutation-symmetric and low-rank.
The equal nonzero means collapse the deterministic part to a rank-one all-ones perturbation with permutation symmetry, used only as an easy case.
the equal-mean case is a minimal nonzero rank-one perturbation with all-ones permutation symmetry, used only as an easy special case
The equal-mean perturbation is the rank-one all-ones matrix, a low-rank highly regular easy case rather than a refutation.
the equal-mean all-ones perturbation is a highly regular minimal nonzero-rank case used as an easy illustration, not to refute a claim
Used as an easy solvable case; the equal-mean perturbation is a permutation-symmetric rank-one/minimal-rank matrix.
The equal-mean matrix is a permutation-invariant all-ones rank-one/minimal-rank perturbation used only as an easy case.
The equal-mean all-ones perturbation is a permutation-symmetric minimal nonzero-rank easy case, not a refutation.

examples.name: Toy deformed Wigner spectrum with one large eigenvalue of A
examples.note: Taking A with one eigenvalue 10 and the rest split between 1 and 2 illustrates the deterministic limiting density and the emergence of a single outlier.
examples.engagement_reason: He instantiates A with eigenvalues 10, 1, and 2 and reasons through the resulting deformed spectrum and the single outlier.

passes.reason: The toy deformation has a single spike at the top of the spectrum producing an outlying extreme eigenvalue.
The example is a spiked deformed Wigner model whose singled-out large eigenvalue produces a spectral outlier at the edge.

The example is a standard deformed Wigner model with an isolated large top eigenvalue producing a spectral outlier.
The toy deformation has a collapsed few-atom bulk plus one separated top spike, producing a spectral-edge outlier.
the toy deformation is organized around a separated largest eigenvalue producing an outlier at the spectral edge
The toy A has a collapsed spectrum with large multiplicities and one isolated large eigenvalue producing an outlier.
the toy deformation is built around a single unusually large spectral spike producing an outlier at the edge
Used to illustrate the deformed Wigner picture; the isolated large eigenvalue of A is an extremal spike producing an outlier.
The toy model has a single separated top eigenvalue producing an outlying spectral extreme, used illustratively.
The separated top eigenvalue is used as the outlier obstruction to blanket eigenvector delocalization.

examples.name: Diagonal A and GOE reducible cases
examples.note: The diagonal deterministic deformation and the GOE case are used as contrast cases where independence/isotropy or rotational invariance makes the problem more tractable.
examples.engagement_reason: He checks why the diagonal-A case retains the useful properties and explains the GOE case by rotating A to diagonal form.

passes.reason: These are tractable reducible cases, with the GOE case relying on maximal orthogonal invariance rather than serving as a failure example.
Diagonal A is a coordinate-decoupled reduction and the GOE case is singled out by orthogonal invariance, used only as a tractable special case.
Diagonal A removes off-diagonal perturbation structure, while the GOE case is tractable because of its orthogonal invariance.
the GOE/diagonal reductions are special tractable cases, with the GOE part distinguished by orthogonal invariance rather than serving adversarially
The GOE case is singled out by orthogonal invariance, which makes reduction to diagonal A possible.
Diagonal A removes generic off-diagonal structure while GOE has orthogonal invariance, and both are cited as tractable reducible cases.
the diagonal deformation removes off-diagonal coupling while the GOE case is reducible by its orthogonal invariance
The diagonal-A case removes off-diagonal structure, while the GOE case is reducible by its orthogonal invariance.
the diagonal-A and GOE cases are tractable because GOE rotational invariance reduces the problem to a diagonal form
Used as reducible special cases; the GOE case has maximal orthogonal invariance enabling diagonalization of A.
These are solvable reduced/high-regularity families: diagonal A removes off-diagonal structure and GOE is orthogonally invariant.
The diagonal-A case is coordinate-decoupled and the GOE case is reducible by rotational symmetry.

examples.name: Stock-price covariance and common-factor model
examples.note: Repeated observations of stock-price trends and common movement of some stocks motivate the centered sample covariance matrix and the signal-plus-noise PCA interpretation.
examples.engagement_reason: He develops the stock-price covariance model, plugs in the linear-factor assumption, and derives the sample covariance form and TT* covariance.

passes.reason: The common-factor covariance model reduces dependence to a low-rank signal added to noise.
The stock model has a low-rank common factor embedded in isotropic noise rather than a generic covariance structure.
The common-factor stock model is identity noise plus a low-rank signal covariance, used as motivation for PCA.
the common-factor stock covariance is a low-rank factor added to isotropic noise, used as motivation rather than to disprove anything
The common-factor covariance is identity noise plus a rank-one signal, with spherical symmetry off the signal direction.
The stock common-factor covariance is a low-rank signal added to noise, serving as motivating structure rather than a refutation.
the common-factor covariance is a minimal rank-one spike over isotropic noise, not an adversarial example
The stock model is a rank-one common-factor covariance over isotropic noise, used as motivation rather than as a failure case.
the common-factor stock model has isotropic noise plus a rank-one signal component rather than a generic covariance structure
Used as motivating statistical model; a latent common-factor covariance setup is a standard representative application, not a structural exception here.
The stock common-factor covariance is a standard low-rank-signal model motivating PCA rather than refuting a claim.
The common-factor covariance has a low-rank signal component and is used as motivating application.

examples.name: One-signal, unit-noise spiked covariance picture
examples.note: With all noise eigenvalues equal to one and a single signal, the speaker displays the outlier threshold and explicit outlier-location formula as a concrete instance of the eigenvalue theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes to unit noise with one signal, states the outlier threshold, gives the outlier-location formula, and explains how to invert it for the signal.

passes.reason: The one-signal unit-noise spiked covariance is a minimal rank-one signal model with an isotropic nonsignal subspace.
The one-signal unit-noise spiked covariance is a rank-one deformation of identity with a top outlier eigenvalue.
The one-signal unit-noise spiked covariance has maximally regular noise and a single separated top outlier.
the one-signal unit-noise spiked covariance has a highly regular repeated-noise spectrum and one extreme spike/outlier
The one-signal unit-noise spiked covariance model is the minimal rank-one spike case and its sample spike appears as an edge outlier.
The one-signal unit-noise model is a rank-one spike over identity noise whose visible signal is the top outlier beyond the bulk edge.
the one-signal unit-noise spiked covariance model is the first nontrivial rank-one spike with rotationally regular noise
The one-signal unit-noise spiked covariance model has a minimal finite-rank signal and an outlying top eigenvalue beyond the bulk.
the one-signal unit-noise spiked covariance model is an isotropic rank-one spike giving a largest outlier eigenvalue
Used as the basic spiked-covariance picture; one rank-one signal over identity noise is minimal-spike and spectrally outlying.
Unit noise plus one spike is an isotropic-noise rank-one spiked case with a separated outlier.

examples.name: Two nearby outliers and eigenvector mixing
examples.note: The case of two close outliers illustrates how empirical eigenvectors mix inside the two-dimensional signal plane while their projected norms and mutual orthogonality are deterministic.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the two-close-outlier situation by describing how the sample eigenvectors project and mix in the two-signal plane.

passes.reason: Two nearby outliers form a low-rank spiked situation at a small-gap limiting regime where eigenvectors mix.
Two nearby spikes form a finite-dimensional near-collision of outlier eigenvalues, causing non-generic eigenvector mixing.
Two nearby outliers form a nearly degenerate extreme spectral cluster, causing eigenvector mixing in the signal plane.
two very nearby outliers form an extreme near-collision of eigenvalues, producing a nearly degenerate signal plane and eigenvector mixing
Two nearly coincident outliers form a near-degenerate critical spectral cluster where eigenvectors mix in the signal plane.
Two very nearby outliers form a near-degenerate limiting spectral cluster that causes eigenvector mixing.
nearby outliers form a near-collision limiting case where the individual eigenvector directions collapse into a mixed signal plane

Used to describe clustered-spike behavior; nearby outliers are extremal spectral points with near eigenvalue-degeneracy.

The nearly colliding outliers form a low-rank near-degenerate hard case where individual eigenvectors mix.

examples.name: Sparse set of related stocks
examples.note: If only a small subset of stocks supports the signal vector, the delocalization theorem explains why large coordinates of the empirical eigenvector reveal that subset.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies the delocalization/eigenvector result to a sparse related-stocks signal to show how nonzero stock components can be identified.

passes.reason: The related-stock signal is sparse, with only a much smaller support of nonzero coordinates.
The related-stock signal is supported on a much smaller coordinate set, so the vector is structurally sparse.

examples.name: Single-entry matrix perturbation and resolvents
examples.note: Changing one entry of a matrix is used to illustrate why relating two resolvents directly is difficult and why isotropic control from arbitrary directions is needed.
examples.engagement_reason: He poses the single-entry perturbation of X as an illustrative difficulty for resolvents but does not compute or derive the resolvent relation for it.
